title: Breaking the n‾√ Barrier: New Parallel Algorithms for Finding a Matroid Basis
speaker: Aaron (Louie) Putterman
abstract:
Over 40 years ago, Karp, Upfal, and Wigderson posed a central open question in parallel computation: how many adaptive rounds are needed to find a basis of a matroid using only independence queries? Their pioneering work gave an upper bound of O(n‾√) rounds and a lower bound of roughly Ω(n1/3) rounds---bounds that have stood unchanged ever since.
In this talk, I will present recent progress on this classic problem. We give a new parallel algorithm that, with high probability, finds a matroid basis in O(n3/7) rounds, breaking the long-standing O(n‾√) barrier. For the important special case of partition matroids, we obtain an optimal O(n1/3)
-round algorithm, settling their complexity. Our approach introduces a new matroid decomposition technique which may be of independent interest, and also yields faster parallel algorithms for the classic matroid intersection problem, among others. 
Based on joint work with Sanjeev Khanna and Junkai Song (Penn).

proof_stages.label: Matroid basis problem and oracle baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines matroids via hereditary and exchange axioms, introduces circuits, bases and rank, formulates basis finding with independence-oracle access, and recalls the sequential greedy algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Parallel adaptive query model and sqrt(n) algorithm
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the adaptive-round model with polynomially many parallel oracle queries per round and presents the Karp-Upfal-Wigderson block-prefix algorithm that processes or deletes about sqrt(n) elements per round.

proof_stages.label: Prior gap and new randomized upper bound
proof_stages.summary: The known randomized lower bound of roughly n^{1/3} rounds and the new randomized n^{3/7}-round upper bound are stated, with the plan to first understand partition matroids and then generalize to arbitrary matroids.

proof_stages.label: Partition-matroid lower bound and variance obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The Karp-Upfal-Wigderson staircase partition-matroid lower-bound intuition is reviewed, and the speaker explains why a naive sqrt(n)-level version fails because standard deviations let queries distinguish many adjacent budget levels at once.

proof_stages.label: Partition-matroid recovery algorithm and circuit-trigger growth
proof_stages.summary: A random-permutation procedure recovers parts causing first dependence, the circuit-trigger parameter alpha is introduced as the median first-dependence time, and a growth-rate lemma shows that peeled sets of comparable size have quadratically increasing alpha values, yielding about n^{1/3} rounds.

proof_stages.label: General-matroid parts and decomposition
proof_stages.summary: For arbitrary matroids, a part is defined using high first-circuit containment probability plus nontrivial element marginals, and these parts can be found and peeled to obtain an analogous decomposition with the same alpha-growth behavior.

proof_stages.label: Independent-set and deletion progress subroutines
proof_stages.summary: The final algorithm processes each recovered part by a case analysis: large alpha yields a large independent set, small alpha yields many deletable redundant elements, and iterating these subroutines gives the improved parallel basis-finding bound.

examples.name: Graphic matroid: forests and cycles
examples.note: A graph's edge sets are independent when acyclic, so bases become spanning forests and circuits become simple cycles, making the abstract matroid notions concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives what bases and circuits become in the graphic matroid, identifying bases as spanning forests and arguing that circuits are single simple cycles rather than unions of cycles by minimality.

examples.name: Linear matroid: vector-space independence
examples.note: Vectors with linear independence give the familiar case where bases are linear-algebra bases, and the span picture explains why the first dependent prefix contains a redundant element.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the linear-matroid analogy, deriving that a newly dependent prefix means the new vector lies in the span of the previous ones and identifying bases/circuits in vector-space terms.

examples.name: Uniform matroid with budget b
examples.note: The rule that every set of size at most b is independent illustrates bases and circuits, and later shows that the circuit-trigger parameter is simply b+1.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the definitions to the uniform matroid, deriving bases as size-b sets, circuits as size b+1 sets, and later computing the circuit-trigger parameter as b+1.

passes.reason: A uniform matroid is the maximally symmetric rank-budget matroid where independence depends only on cardinality.

examples.name: Hypothetical square-root staircase partition matroid
examples.note: The attempted sqrt(n)-part staircase lower-bound instance reveals that sampling variance lets one learn about a window of roughly sqrt(i) parts at once, producing the key quadratic-growth intuition.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the square-root staircase partition instance, computing expected samples per part, the standard deviation, and explaining how this lets one learn blocks beyond the next part.

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the prefix-query construction on the six-element two-part partition toy model, tracing how the first dependent query reveals the offending part and then the whole part.

examples.name: Graphic-matroid part as shortest cycles
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the general-matroid notion of a part would correspond in a graphic matroid to the union of the shortest simple cycles, analogously to first circuits under random permutations.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only describes the graphic-matroid part informally as the union of shortest cycles and compares it to a Karger-style bound, without carrying out a concrete computation or construction on a graph.
